sentences.text: We were talking about Normandy--
Yes.
I was telling you.
And let me end-- I'll end up this with the 82nd.
So, the 80-- Eisenhower finally said, you're going into invasion.
We're going to take off.
You're going-- they had sent us down to the marshalling yards at Plymouth, which was the assembly area for the invasion. 82nd-- they sent them to wherever they sent them.
They stayed there.
And that's where they mounted their planes and they were taken over.
And I remember seeing, that morning we-- that morning, before we went over, that they would-- there was a-- each one of those-- each one of their C-47s was pulling two gliders.
And they were just-- air was filled with them.
So and they say the paratroopers were inside the 47, the C-47s.
And the glider troops were in the other one.
So they were on their way to Normandy.
We got on boats at night.
And we were on our way to Normandy.
And I've never seen so many boats in my whole life.
I mean, there were just thousands of them out there.
And the two big battleships were making runs down the coast when we got there, and-- and-- and bombing everything back up to try to soften up and get rid of the pill boxes.

We-- our job was to go ashore and to pull-- to pull-- take tape and clear paths up through the-- up through the hillside, so-- and get rid of all the anti-personnel mines that had been sewed in there.
So that-- and with the tapes, the soldiers coming ashore behind us would know that this was a safe way to go up to the-- to the hills up above us.
We came into Omaha, what they call Omaha Red.
And that's what-- that's what our jobs were and that's what we did.
We saw the Rangers when they were going up the side of the cliff.
And I tell you, that was amazing.
I mean, every place you turn, people were-- there was fire coming across the beaches, just like it was sowing seeds.
And I remember, I had two fellows, one on each side of me.
And we hid behind a-- a-- a structure, one of the-- those fixtures they put on the beach to keep the ships from coming ashore and the tanks from coming ashore.
We hid behind one.
You know what, you felt like if you hid behind a-- a-- a lump of-- you know, a bit of sand that that will protect you.
Any place you could find a place to dive in, you did.
And we went behind this.
And you know, the most amazing thing-- I'll always remember, the guy on either side of me was killed, and I lived.
And I-- you know, it's wild.
You say to yourself, how could they miss me and they got those two.
But anyway, we finally got up to the-- we got up to the assembly area that had been pre-designated.
And we-- we pulled them on.
You know, a lot of our guys got killed while that was going on, because obviously they were exposed at the time we were pulling.
But we lost a lot of fellas.
In fact, by-- in about three or four hours, we had lost all of our officers.
Every one of them was dead who'd come ashore with us.
And because white officers had a band-- a white band, a vertical band-- behind the ears.
And we had a-- non-commissioned officers-- we had a horizontal band.
I had covered mine up, just very carefully, because I said, look.
You know, anybody that looks different attracts someone.
And so I covered mine up with-- with the garnish that I had on and my nets.
And-- and I said, look-- and I took all my stripes off.
Because it wasn't any sense to identify-- everybody knew who I was.
OK, what happened was after Normandy, we went north-- went south to Saint-Lo.
And from Saint-Lo, we went southeast to Paris.
And we're walking.
I think I walked all the way across Europe.
But anyway, we went to-- [LAUGHS] and we got to-- out of the hedgerows, once we were able to fight our way out of the hedgerows.
Because we were-- and we went to Paris.
And then from Paris, we went down to Bois-le-Duc, to the Meuse river, in that area, and from-- which was a area under General Patton.
And we-- and it-- that was about the time the winter was starting.
And it was-- it was-- it was just cold.
And we-- we went up to [?
Malmedy, ?] and the Germans broke through in the Bulge.
And we-- they had told-- they instructed all of us to stop every carriage, every cart, everything that was coming through, and be sure that you-- that there were no Germans in them.
Because the Germans were coming looking like farmers, and all this sort of thing, and infiltrating behind our lines.
And the only thing I remember-- I-- I was in a jeep, with a 30-- I had a Thompson submachine gun.
And I re-- and I was-- we were just running to see what we could find.
We were checking our area.
And I saw these two horses drawing this big cart.
So I stopped them.
And I said-- they spoke English.
And they spoke very beautiful English.
It sounded like British, really.
So I says, hey.
And it looked like farmers.
So I'm saying to myself, this doesn't make any sense.
So I finally said-- I said, hey, how's the Yankees doing?
And he says-- the New York Yankees, the baseball team.
I said, have you seen anything about how they're doing, how they did this year?
And he said, yeah, we saw-- yes.
He said something about you know, many times I've seen them.
I've gone to our stadium in Chicago, the Chicago Yankees.
I says, what?
And I talked-- I told the guys-- told them-- beckoned one of the guys to slip around behind the thing.
And I just stepped back and opened up with my Thompson submachine gun into-- you know, into the hay.
There were 25 guys dead in there.
They're all German soldiers, and they all were dressed in uniform.
Yes, they were slipping around.
They was trying to slip through the-- to get in the-- and the only reason why is that two things stopped me, was one, because he spoke English so well.
And the other one-- that he's talking about the [LAUGHS] New York Yankees.
That's what I was about to say.
We left-- we left-- we left-- once the Bulge was over, we-- we moved to Aachen.
We went to Aachen, Duren, Euskirchen, and Cologne.
And then it was-- it was during Cologne-- then we went south to [?
Adanac ?] and [?
Adernoy, ?] along the Rhine.
It was during that period that I was asked-- well, it was really after that.
Because we went over into central Germany.

And at some point while we're in the campaign-- it was getting near spring, as I remember-- I was told to-- to go down to Munich to teach these guys, a group-- there was a new mine the Germans were using.
And it was a-- a plastic, nonmetallic mine.
And they told me to go down.
I had learned how to do this.
And I was deactivating them.
And they said to go down and train a group of engineers, go down near Munich, on how to deactivate the new mines.
So that's where I took off with.
I took off with a whole bunch of mines.
And I had the fuses in my pocket, but a whole bunch of mines in the back of my jeep.
And-- Who went with you?
I-- I had one fellow with me, my driver.
And-- and the two of us were going.
And we picked up an officer along the way, a-- a white captain, who bummed a ride with us.
And he said, what's this back here with me?
I said [LAUGHS] land-- landmines.
He said, stop the jeep.
[LAUGHTER] I said, I got the fuses in my pocket.
He-- I don't care where you got the fuses.
Stop the jeep!
So I stopped and he got out.
And that's the last person we had with us.
So I went down to-- and that's when we went to Munich.
Yes it was.
It was a black unit that was attached to or assigned to-- I'm not sure what they were-- but they were assigned to the 183rd infantry.
And when I got down there, you know, they-- we were-- I was training them.
And then they got orders to move and take this camp.
And we thought-- and they got-- you know, everybody got-- they got their weapons and all the heavy weapons they had.
They had-- they had bazookas and-- and heavy machine guns.
And we went in and we were going to take this camp.
And they had a half-track also.
And when we got to the camp, we didn't see any guards or anything.
No.
Oh no, no, no.
They told us how to get there.
I think there was-- with us, there were two platoons, which means that there were probably a couple of hundred guys with us when we went up there.
We're all armed, yes.
Because we were-- we thought we were going to be in a war, you know, in a-- you know, in a skirmish with-- with the Germans, who ran the camp.
What we assumed the camp was was an army military camp that we were going to, and that we were there to take the camp.
Because they said take the camp.
We were confused a bit because we never had-- you know, as a combat engineers, our job was never to go and assault something.
That was the infantry's job.
We supported the infantry in many times in that sort of thing, and we fought defensive wars.
You know, somebody's building a bridge, somebody tries to take the bridge, we try to-- we stop them, if we can.
That sort of thing.
I don't really know.
There were-- not where we went in, there wasn't anybody else.
But I don't know.
From the outside, it-- as I remember, it was-- it was a-- it was-- it was not a wall.
It was-- it was like a wire fence around it.
And there was a big gate.
And--
I know something in German.
And later on, one of the prisoners told us that was the name of the camp that we saw.
But at the time we were going in, it just-- there was some German language up there and that's all we knew.
And we broke through the gate with the half-track and got inside.
And when we got inside, there were no soldiers around.
But yet, I heard firing on the other side of the camp somewhere.
It was a big camp.
So we heard firing.
Oh heavens, I don't know.
It must have been the south because we were-- we left Munich and went straight up-- straight up to the camp.
I was feeling it was the south-- it was the south end, as I remember.
But I do know that we heard firing.
So we knew that somebody else was somewhere.
But what happened was as we got inside, these people came out of these barracks-like buildings, with their striped uniforms on, and just in devastated shape.
You know, they were just-- and they were-- [SIGHS] oh, God.
They-- they had all kinds of sores on them, many of them.
Their hair was-- and they had lice on them.
They were just in terrible shape.
At that point, you know what I thought?
We thought-- we thought.
We thought that they were people who had violated German laws and had been sent to prison.
Because they looked like people on the chain gang in their uniforms.
They looked a lot-- it reminded you of the chain gang uniforms.
They were this different color, but they reminded you of chain gang, because they were sort of gray and black.
But we-- so we were trying to push them off of us, because I don't know who these people are.
And they-- and they are in such bad shape, physically, that we kept pushing them away.
And to this day, I am very-- I feel very badly about that.
Because we didn't know-- and once I found-- and the only reason why I found out who they were-- I'm telling-- is that there was a fellow who-- one of the fellows came out who spoke English.
And he said, are you Americans?
And I said, yes.
He said, thank God.
And he hit the ground and started to pray.
And-- I-- I stood beside him.
And he explained to me that he was a rabbi.
And I said, can you tell me what this place is and why those bodies are stacked up against the fence?
You-- you saw bodies stacked up at the fence?
Yes, yes.
No, they were stripped.
Yes.
And we-- he said that it was a concentration camp.
And I said, what?
And he said, no, it's a death camp.
And I said what-- what-- what are you talking about?
And then he began to explain to me what went on there, and the ovens-- what they were for.
Oh, yes.
And he explained what they were.

And he explained-- and then the most barbaric thing I saw, I think the thing that I will never forget, was there were these little rows of piles of things that were-- that were sort of the same distance apart.
They were people's gold teeth that they'd taken out of people's mouths.
And that's what he said.
I said, who-- what kind of human being would do this?
And he said, well, they take them out before they put them in the furnace.
I said, it makes no-- I said, why is this-- why is this happening?
And I said, what did you guys do to cause yourself to get this kind of treatment?
He says, we didn't do anything.
We're Jews.
I said, it makes no sense!
He says, you're right, it doesn't make any sense.
But we are being-- we've been getting killed because we were Jews.
And as I sat there looking at him, you know, I said, you know, I think I began to understand.

I said, I remember my mother telling me, and my grandmother particularly telling me about, what happened to black people in slavery, and how we were-- we were totally at somebody else's mercy, that [LAUGHS] you get up and go to bed in somebody else's orders.
If somebody wanted to kill you, they could kill you without having to pay any price for it.
And they slaughtered an awful lot of people that way.
And I think I understand slavery.
He said, well this is-- and I think he mentioned something about slavery.
And I-- that's what I hung onto as we were talking.
He thought it was a slave camp, that's what he said.
And that's why-- we were going through this thing about slavery.
And I understood that.
I was the only one he was talking to.
The rest of the guys was-- they were still-- some of the guys wanted to feed the people.
And they were being-- the officer said, don't feed them because you might make them sick.
Because once-- we were beginning to learn now who they are.
Because various people, other people, were speaking to them also.
And the people were so nice, to-- you know, they were so happy about us being there.
And finally, we came to grips with that because a couple of my officers began to explain to us, to the guys, about what kind of camp this was.
But he told us to be on the alert because there may be German soldiers still around.
No, not where we were.
We didn't explore any place.
I went in one with the rabbi, only because he wanted to pick up something.
And he wanted to be-- and I'll protect-- I said, well, I'll protect you when you go.
And we went in.
And--
There were just rows of-- of-- there were beds that were just very, very rustic kind of things.
They weren't nothing of comfort.
There was all kinds of people's clothing material around on the floor, you know, behind the beds and all.
But there were just long rows of beds on two-- with an aisle down the middle.
Oh, Jesus.
You know, to this day-- well, in the Army, I'd smelled death before then.
Because I mean, we did-- a lot of times, we smelled death.
But it was nothing quite like this.
It was just the smell of death, of dead people.
And the barracks had the smell of just human smells, of people, you know, unwashed, unclothed.
Whatever you want to call it, it was all there.
There were women there too.
Yeah, well they came out-- women who came out.
And I guess they were in there.
I don't know why they were there.
There were children.
There were old people.
There were all kinds of folk that were there.
Because among the dead, there were all kinds of people.
You know, old folks, young people, women, children, what have you, were stacked up along the wall.
Because those are the people we buried.
We dug the holes.
The Army told us where they wanted us to do it.
We were assigned-- after we were there for the rest of that day, we came back there.
We were ordered the next day to go back.
Went back to the camp we came from.
Which was only about-- I'd say about 20 miles at the most.
And we went back.
And the next day, they told us to bring our heavy bulldozers back up to the camp.
And they want us to dig the holes to bury the dead.
And we brought-- they brought two D-8s-- a D-8 and a D-12 bulldozer.
And we dug the holes with long slit trenches.
And there were some people there from Army Graves Registration.
We called it Graves Registration.
They're the guys who-- when people died in the Army, they were the guys who identified the dead person.
And they sent the-- took one dog-- one dog tag off, and send it back-- you know, that sort of thing.
These were the people that we saw.
And plus some other fellas from headquarters we didn't know-- they all came from the Army headquarters.
And they lectured us, at that point, on what-- what kind of camp it was, what had been going on.
And there was a fellow from Army headquarters who did that, from Eisenhower's headquarters.
They said he came all the way from there.
But anyway, he told us what it was.

And he told us what we were seeing and who the people were and said that one of the things they want us to do-- now they wanted to be careful that any identifying marks on people, their people would take care of, and not to bury anybody until we were ordered to bury that body.
And they would take the rest of it.
And they did.
And when they said the bodies were ready to go in, in a block, we'd take them and push them into the hole.
Some of them were in a bad state of deterioration.
Most of them were, really.
I-- They--
No, they hadn't been--
They hadn't been burned, no.
But they were all-- you know, they were dead.
And they were stiff.
You know, they were-- you know, they'd been dead for a while.
Military people.
There were some people from military headquarters who-- who were handling the whole thing.
I didn't see any.
No.
No, no.
No, no.
Uh-uh.
We just dug slit-- we dug this long-- if they did, we didn't know anything about it.
We dug these long trenches.
And we put the bodies-- pushed the bodies into the trenches, and covered them over.
After they were--
Oh, I would say it must have been hundreds, several hundred, at least.
Where we were, we were the only ones doing it.
Now they-- you know, it's a big camp.
I don't know what was going on on the other side.
But I know where we were, we were the only ones assigned to do that job.
They were gone.
The next day, they were taking them out, started putting them on trucks and taking them.
I didn't-- the Army-- at that point, the army is in control of the whole thing and-- from headquarters.
We're no longer-- it's no longer us.
It's-- it's headquarters, who's come down.
They came down with the vehicles.
They came down with the doctors.
They came down with nurse.
They brought a whole cadre of medical people with them.
And they were caring for the people who were-- who were in the camp at that point.
And far as I know, they, you know, they were doing [? good-- ?] they-- they were feeding them.
I do remember they were feeding them something like a soup.
It was a little heavier.
And they were-- they were-- they had brought the containers for people to eat with and all that sort of thing.
So they feed them.
Because they had told us not to feed them.
Because we were carrying field rations, you know.
And they said that the field rations would make them sick.
And we certainly didn't want anybody to be sick.
I did have water.
And I had my canteen.
And they said I could give them a little water but be careful, don't let them drink too much.
Yeah.
Oh, I saw-- you know, this-- now the person who sent me the menorah, who is dead now-- evidently, I talked to them.
I talked to several people after I talked to the rabbi [INAUDIBLE] know who they are.
I don't remember who they were.
I know I talked to one woman-- that's interesting.
I talked to this woman, who was a child, a young child.
And I told her who I was.
I was on my knees, talking to her.
And I told her my name.
You know, it was just a nice kid who was just-- and she wasn't in too good of shape.
But she was in better shape than most of them for some reason.
And we talked.
And I-- and it was over.
One day, I was in Boston here.
And I went to a meeting of the survivors.
It was several years ago-- must have been 10 or 12 years ago.
And this woman-- she was at the fair yesterday.
But this woman screamed and ran over to me and said, I know you, I know you, I know you.
And I'm saying, this woman doesn't know me at all.
What's she talking about?
[LAUGHS] And she identified herself as the little girl I talked to at Dachau.
And I was just amazed how she would remember me.
She said, I know you by your eyes.
And I said, isn't that amazing?
How little was she then?
Oh, I'd say she's about 10 or 11 at the oldest.
That's what [INAUDIBLE].
Oh, I had guys who broke down and got sick.
I had fellows who cried.
You know, these are just all kinds-- we were all affected by it.
I just got angrier.
I just sat there angry.
I just-- you know, I wanted to kill somebody.

And it just-- you know, it was just sheer-- I just couldn't believe anybody-- you know the thing that bothered me most of all is I talked to people who were gentle people, who are very bright people, who were intelligent people.

And to degrade another human being this way, you know, to take away a person's dignity to this extent-- and I think I could relate to that, from the Purdues and all the places I had been, that I just felt that somehow this is awful.
You know, people shouldn't have to live this way.
Nobody should do another human being this way.
That here are people who [? after ?] all those things that everybody thinks people should do to be a decent person and suddenly, through no fault of their own, to be destroyed this way.
And that-- how could these men, people, say that they're civilized?
What did-- how did some of these, you know, buddies or some of the others take out their anger?
Oh, I had guys firing in the ground.
They were doing all sorts of things.
They would unload their weapons in the ground.
They-- because we wanted to fight somebody at that point.
And a lot them-- some of the guys just sat staring.
You know, they was-- but everybody was affected.
There was nobody who took this lightly.
No.
I saw the-- I saw the furnaces.
Yes.
There was-- they were stacked, like you know, one on top, two or-- two deep, in a long row.
And those I saw.
No, I didn't see any of those.
No.
No, we were-- we were just confined to do just what I said there.
You know, we weren't moving any further than we were, because we were under the officers' direction.
And those people came out so we had our hands full just dealing--
--with the crowd, the number of people.
And once we knew who they were, just trying to help them as much as we could.
Yeah, the living.
Onto the trucks.
And we brought stretchers for those who couldn't move.
They brought stretchers.
We helped them to bring the people out on the-- get the people on stretchers, and all the other things we were doing.
So we were very busy.
And we didn't really care about the camp at all.
You know, and so that's why-- we kept saying, we ought to blow it up.
And the officer said we couldn't blow it up.
He said, I think that's what ought to happen.
It ought to be destroyed.
